lines: Always obedient to your Grace's will,
I come to know your pleasure.
fullText: Always obedient to your Grace's will, I come to know your pleasure.

lines: Now, good my lord,
Let there be some more test made of my mettle
Before so noble and so great a figure
Be stamped upon it.
fullText: Now, good my lord, Let there be some more test made of my mettle Before so noble and so great a figure Be stamped upon it.

lines: Yet give leave, my lord,
That we may bring you something on the way.
fullText: Yet give leave, my lord, That we may bring you something on the way.

lines: The heavens give safety to your purposes.
fullText: The heavens give safety to your purposes.

lines: 'Tis so with me. Let us withdraw together,
And we may soon our satisfaction have
Touching that point.
fullText: 'Tis so with me. Let us withdraw together, And we may soon our satisfaction have Touching that point.

lines: We must not make a scarecrow of the law,
Setting it up to fear the birds of prey,
And let it keep one shape till custom make it
Their perch and not their terror.
fullText: We must not make a scarecrow of the law, Setting it up to fear the birds of prey, And let it keep one shape till custom make it Their perch and not their terror.

lines: 'Tis one thing to be tempted, Escalus,
Another thing to fall. I not deny
The jury passing on the prisoner's life
May in the sworn twelve have a thief or two
Guiltier than him they try. What's open made to
justice,
That justice seizes. What knows the laws
That thieves do pass on thieves? 'Tis very pregnant,
The jewel that we find, we stoop and take 't
Because we see it; but what we do not see,
We tread upon and never think of it.
You may not so extenuate his offense
For I have had such faults; but rather tell me,
When I that censure him do so offend,
Let mine own judgment pattern out my death,
And nothing come in partial. Sir, he must die.
fullText: 'Tis one thing to be tempted, Escalus, Another thing to fall. I not deny The jury passing on the prisoner's life May in the sworn twelve have a thief or two Guiltier than him they try. What's open made to justice, That justice seizes. What knows the laws That thieves do pass on thieves? 'Tis very pregnant, The jewel that we find, we stoop and take 't Because we see it; but what we do not see, We tread upon and never think of it. You may not so extenuate his offense For I have had such faults; but rather tell me, When I that censure him do so offend, Let mine own judgment pattern out my death, And nothing come in partial. Sir, he must die.

lines: Where is the Provost?
fullText: Where is the Provost?

lines: See that Claudio
Be executed by nine tomorrow morning.
Bring him his confessor, let him be prepared,
For that's the utmost of his pilgrimage.
fullText: See that Claudio Be executed by nine tomorrow morning. Bring him his confessor, let him be prepared, For that's the utmost of his pilgrimage.

lines: How now, sir, what's your name? And what's
the matter?
fullText: How now, sir, what's your name? And what's the matter?

lines: Benefactors? Well, what benefactors are they?
Are they not malefactors?
fullText: Benefactors? Well, what benefactors are they? Are they not malefactors?

lines: Go to. What quality are they of?
Elbow is your name? Why dost thou not speak,
Elbow?
fullText: Go to. What quality are they of? Elbow is your name? Why dost thou not speak, Elbow?

lines: What are you, sir?
fullText: What are you, sir?

lines: This will last out a night in Russia
When nights are longest there. I'll take my leave,
And leave you to the hearing of the cause,
Hoping you'll find good cause to whip them all.
fullText: This will last out a night in Russia When nights are longest there. I'll take my leave, And leave you to the hearing of the cause, Hoping you'll find good cause to whip them all.

lines: Now, what's the matter, provost?
fullText: Now, what's the matter, provost?

lines: Did not I tell thee yea? Hadst thou not order?
Why dost thou ask again?
fullText: Did not I tell thee yea? Hadst thou not order? Why dost thou ask again?

lines: Go to. Let that be mine.
Do you your office, or give up your place
And you shall well be spared.
fullText: Go to. Let that be mine. Do you your office, or give up your place And you shall well be spared.

lines: Dispose of her
To some more fitter place, and that with speed.
fullText: Dispose of her To some more fitter place, and that with speed.

lines: Hath he a sister?
fullText: Hath he a sister?

lines: Well, let her be admitted.
See you the fornicatress be removed.
Let her have needful but not lavish means.
There shall be order for 't.
fullText: Well, let her be admitted. See you the fornicatress be removed. Let her have needful but not lavish means. There shall be order for 't.

lines: Stay a little while. You're welcome.
What's your will?
fullText: Stay a little while. You're welcome. What's your will?

lines: Well, what's your
suit?
fullText: Well, what's your suit?

lines: Well, the matter?
fullText: Well, the matter?

lines: Condemn the fault, and not the actor of it?
Why, every fault's condemned ere it be done.
Mine were the very cipher of a function
To fine the faults whose fine stands in record
And let go by the actor.
fullText: Condemn the fault, and not the actor of it? Why, every fault's condemned ere it be done. Mine were the very cipher of a function To fine the faults whose fine stands in record And let go by the actor.

lines: Maiden, no remedy.
fullText: Maiden, no remedy.

lines: I will not do 't.
fullText: I will not do 't.

lines: Look what I will not, that I cannot do.
fullText: Look what I will not, that I cannot do.

lines: He's sentenced. 'Tis too late.
fullText: He's sentenced. 'Tis too late.

lines: Pray you begone.
fullText: Pray you begone.

lines: Your brother is a forfeit of the law,
And you but waste your words.
fullText: Your brother is a forfeit of the law, And you but waste your words.

lines: Be you content, fair maid.
It is the law, not I, condemn your brother.
Were he my kinsman, brother, or my son,
It should be thus with him. He must die tomorrow.
fullText: Be you content, fair maid. It is the law, not I, condemn your brother. Were he my kinsman, brother, or my son, It should be thus with him. He must die tomorrow.

lines: The law hath not been dead, though it hath slept.
Those many had not dared to do that evil
If the first that did th' edict infringe
Had answered for his deed. Now 'tis awake,
Takes note of what is done, and, like a prophet,
Looks in a glass that shows what future evils--
Either now, or by remissness new-conceived,
And so in progress to be hatched and born--
Are now to have no successive degrees
But, ere they live, to end.
fullText: The law hath not been dead, though it hath slept. Those many had not dared to do that evil If the first that did th' edict infringe Had answered for his deed. Now 'tis awake, Takes note of what is done, and, like a prophet, Looks in a glass that shows what future evils-- Either now, or by remissness new-conceived, And so in progress to be hatched and born-- Are now to have no successive degrees But, ere they live, to end.

lines: I show it most of all when I show justice,
For then I pity those I do not know,
Which a dismissed offense would after gall,
And do him right that, answering one foul wrong,
Lives not to act another. Be satisfied;
Your brother dies tomorrow; be content.
fullText: I show it most of all when I show justice, For then I pity those I do not know, Which a dismissed offense would after gall, And do him right that, answering one foul wrong, Lives not to act another. Be satisfied; Your brother dies tomorrow; be content.

lines: Why do you put these sayings upon me?
fullText: Why do you put these sayings upon me?

lines: She speaks, and 'tis such sense
That my sense breeds with it.
Fare you well.
fullText: She speaks, and 'tis such sense That my sense breeds with it. Fare you well.

lines: I will bethink me. Come again tomorrow.
fullText: I will bethink me. Come again tomorrow.

lines: How? Bribe me?
fullText: How? Bribe me?

lines: Well, come to me tomorrow.
fullText: Well, come to me tomorrow.

lines: Amen.
For I am that way going to temptation
Where prayers cross.
fullText: Amen. For I am that way going to temptation Where prayers cross.

lines: At any time 'fore noon.
fullText: At any time 'fore noon.

lines: From thee, even from thy virtue.
What's this? What's this? Is this her fault or mine?
The tempter or the tempted, who sins most, ha?
Not she, nor doth she tempt; but it is I
That, lying by the violet in the sun,
Do as the carrion does, not as the flower,
Corrupt with virtuous season. Can it be
That modesty may more betray our sense
Than woman's lightness? Having waste ground
enough,
Shall we desire to raze the sanctuary
And pitch our evils there? O fie, fie, fie!
What dost thou, or what art thou, Angelo?
Dost thou desire her foully for those things
That make her good? O, let her brother live.
Thieves for their robbery have authority
When judges steal themselves. What, do I love her
That I desire to hear her speak again
And feast upon her eyes? What is 't I dream on?
O cunning enemy that, to catch a saint,
With saints dost bait thy hook. Most dangerous
Is that temptation that doth goad us on
To sin in loving virtue. Never could the strumpet
With all her double vigor, art and nature,
Once stir my temper, but this virtuous maid
Subdues me quite. Ever till now
When men were fond, I smiled and wondered how.
fullText: From thee, even from thy virtue. What's this? What's this? Is this her fault or mine? The tempter or the tempted, who sins most, ha? Not she, nor doth she tempt; but it is I That, lying by the violet in the sun, Do as the carrion does, not as the flower, Corrupt with virtuous season. Can it be That modesty may more betray our sense Than woman's lightness? Having waste ground enough, Shall we desire to raze the sanctuary And pitch our evils there? O fie, fie, fie! What dost thou, or what art thou, Angelo? Dost thou desire her foully for those things That make her good? O, let her brother live. Thieves for their robbery have authority When judges steal themselves. What, do I love her That I desire to hear her speak again And feast upon her eyes? What is 't I dream on? O cunning enemy that, to catch a saint, With saints dost bait thy hook. Most dangerous Is that temptation that doth goad us on To sin in loving virtue. Never could the strumpet With all her double vigor, art and nature, Once stir my temper, but this virtuous maid Subdues me quite. Ever till now When men were fond, I smiled and wondered how.

lines: When I would pray and think, I think and pray
To several subjects. Heaven hath my empty words,
Whilst my invention, hearing not my tongue,
Anchors on Isabel. God in my mouth,
As if I did but only chew His name,
And in my heart the strong and swelling evil
Of my conception. The state whereon I studied
Is, like a good thing being often read,
Grown sere and tedious. Yea, my gravity,
Wherein--let no man hear me--I take pride,
Could I with boot change for an idle plume
Which the air beats for vain. O place, O form,
How often dost thou with thy case, thy habit,
Wrench awe from fools, and tie the wiser souls
To thy false seeming! Blood, thou art blood.
Let's write "good angel" on the devil's horn.
'Tis not the devil's crest. How now,
who's there?
fullText: When I would pray and think, I think and pray To several subjects. Heaven hath my empty words, Whilst my invention, hearing not my tongue, Anchors on Isabel. God in my mouth, As if I did but only chew His name, And in my heart the strong and swelling evil Of my conception. The state whereon I studied Is, like a good thing being often read, Grown sere and tedious. Yea, my gravity, Wherein--let no man hear me--I take pride, Could I with boot change for an idle plume Which the air beats for vain. O place, O form, How often dost thou with thy case, thy habit, Wrench awe from fools, and tie the wiser souls To thy false seeming! Blood, thou art blood. Let's write "good angel" on the devil's horn. 'Tis not the devil's crest. How now, who's there?

lines: Teach her the way. O heavens,
Why does my blood thus muster to my heart,
Making both it unable for itself
And dispossessing all my other parts
Of necessary fitness?
So play the foolish throngs with one that swoons,
Come all to help him, and so stop the air
By which he should revive. And even so
The general subject to a well-wished king
Quit their own part, and in obsequious fondness
Crowd to his presence, where their untaught love
Must needs appear offense.
fullText: Teach her the way. O heavens, Why does my blood thus muster to my heart, Making both it unable for itself And dispossessing all my other parts Of necessary fitness? So play the foolish throngs with one that swoons, Come all to help him, and so stop the air By which he should revive. And even so The general subject to a well-wished king Quit their own part, and in obsequious fondness Crowd to his presence, where their untaught love Must needs appear offense.

lines: That you might know it would much better please me
Than to demand what 'tis. Your brother cannot live.
fullText: That you might know it would much better please me Than to demand what 'tis. Your brother cannot live.

lines: Yet may he live a while. And it may be
As long as you or I. Yet he must die.
fullText: Yet may he live a while. And it may be As long as you or I. Yet he must die.

lines: Yea.
fullText: Yea.

lines: Ha! Fie, these filthy vices! It were as good
To pardon him that hath from nature stolen
A man already made, as to remit
Their saucy sweetness that do coin God's image
In stamps that are forbid. 'Tis all as easy
Falsely to take away a life true made
As to put metal in restrained means
To make a false one.
fullText: Ha! Fie, these filthy vices! It were as good To pardon him that hath from nature stolen A man already made, as to remit Their saucy sweetness that do coin God's image In stamps that are forbid. 'Tis all as easy Falsely to take away a life true made As to put metal in restrained means To make a false one.

lines: Say you so? Then I shall pose you quickly:
Which had you rather, that the most just law
Now took your brother's life, or, to redeem him,
Give up your body to such sweet uncleanness
As she that he hath stained?
fullText: Say you so? Then I shall pose you quickly: Which had you rather, that the most just law Now took your brother's life, or, to redeem him, Give up your body to such sweet uncleanness As she that he hath stained?

lines: I talk not of your soul. Our compelled sins
Stand more for number than for accompt.
fullText: I talk not of your soul. Our compelled sins Stand more for number than for accompt.

lines: Nay, I'll not warrant that, for I can speak
Against the thing I say. Answer to this:
I, now the voice of the recorded law,
Pronounce a sentence on your brother's life.
Might there not be a charity in sin
To save this brother's life?
fullText: Nay, I'll not warrant that, for I can speak Against the thing I say. Answer to this: I, now the voice of the recorded law, Pronounce a sentence on your brother's life. Might there not be a charity in sin To save this brother's life?

lines: Pleased you to do 't, at peril of your soul,
Were equal poise of sin and charity.
fullText: Pleased you to do 't, at peril of your soul, Were equal poise of sin and charity.

lines: Nay, but hear me.
Your sense pursues not mine. Either you are
ignorant,
Or seem so, crafty, and that's not good.
fullText: Nay, but hear me. Your sense pursues not mine. Either you are ignorant, Or seem so, crafty, and that's not good.

lines: Thus wisdom wishes to appear most bright
When it doth tax itself, as these black masks
Proclaim an enshield beauty ten times louder
Than beauty could, displayed. But mark me.
To be received plain, I'll speak more gross:
Your brother is to die.
fullText: Thus wisdom wishes to appear most bright When it doth tax itself, as these black masks Proclaim an enshield beauty ten times louder Than beauty could, displayed. But mark me. To be received plain, I'll speak more gross: Your brother is to die.

lines: And his offense is so, as it appears,
Accountant to the law upon that pain.
fullText: And his offense is so, as it appears, Accountant to the law upon that pain.

lines: Admit no other way to save his life--
As I subscribe not that, nor any other--
But, in the loss of question, that you, his sister,
Finding yourself desired of such a person
Whose credit with the judge, or own great place,
Could fetch your brother from the manacles
Of the all-binding law, and that there were
No earthly mean to save him but that either
You must lay down the treasures of your body
To this supposed, or else to let him suffer,
What would you do?
fullText: Admit no other way to save his life-- As I subscribe not that, nor any other-- But, in the loss of question, that you, his sister, Finding yourself desired of such a person Whose credit with the judge, or own great place, Could fetch your brother from the manacles Of the all-binding law, and that there were No earthly mean to save him but that either You must lay down the treasures of your body To this supposed, or else to let him suffer, What would you do?

lines: Then must your brother die.
fullText: Then must your brother die.

lines: Were not you then as cruel as the sentence
That you have slandered so?
fullText: Were not you then as cruel as the sentence That you have slandered so?

lines: You seemed of late to make the law a tyrant,
And rather proved the sliding of your brother
A merriment than a vice.
fullText: You seemed of late to make the law a tyrant, And rather proved the sliding of your brother A merriment than a vice.

lines: We are all frail.
fullText: We are all frail.

lines: Nay, women are frail too.
fullText: Nay, women are frail too.

lines: I think it well.
And from this testimony of your own sex,
Since I suppose we are made to be no stronger
Than faults may shake our frames, let me be bold.
I do arrest your words. Be that you are--
That is, a woman. If you be more, you're none.
If you be one, as you are well expressed
By all external warrants, show it now
By putting on the destined livery.
fullText: I think it well. And from this testimony of your own sex, Since I suppose we are made to be no stronger Than faults may shake our frames, let me be bold. I do arrest your words. Be that you are-- That is, a woman. If you be more, you're none. If you be one, as you are well expressed By all external warrants, show it now By putting on the destined livery.

lines: Plainly conceive I love you.
fullText: Plainly conceive I love you.

lines: He shall not, Isabel, if you give me love.
fullText: He shall not, Isabel, if you give me love.

lines: Believe me, on mine honor,
My words express my purpose.
fullText: Believe me, on mine honor, My words express my purpose.

lines: Who will believe thee, Isabel?
My unsoiled name, th' austereness of my life,
My vouch against you, and my place i' th' state
Will so your accusation overweigh
That you shall stifle in your own report
And smell of calumny. I have begun,
And now I give my sensual race the rein.
Fit thy consent to my sharp appetite;
Lay by all nicety and prolixious blushes
That banish what they sue for. Redeem thy brother
By yielding up thy body to my will,
Or else he must not only die the death,
But thy unkindness shall his death draw out
To ling'ring sufferance. Answer me tomorrow,
Or by the affection that now guides me most,
I'll prove a tyrant to him. As for you,
Say what you can, my false o'erweighs your true.
fullText: Who will believe thee, Isabel? My unsoiled name, th' austereness of my life, My vouch against you, and my place i' th' state Will so your accusation overweigh That you shall stifle in your own report And smell of calumny. I have begun, And now I give my sensual race the rein. Fit thy consent to my sharp appetite; Lay by all nicety and prolixious blushes That banish what they sue for. Redeem thy brother By yielding up thy body to my will, Or else he must not only die the death, But thy unkindness shall his death draw out To ling'ring sufferance. Answer me tomorrow, Or by the affection that now guides me most, I'll prove a tyrant to him. As for you, Say what you can, my false o'erweighs your true.

lines: In most uneven and distracted manner. His
actions show much like to madness. Pray heaven his
wisdom be not tainted. And why meet him at the
gates and deliver our authorities there?
fullText: In most uneven and distracted manner. His actions show much like to madness. Pray heaven his wisdom be not tainted. And why meet him at the gates and deliver our authorities there?

lines: And why should we proclaim it in an hour
before his entering, that if any crave redress of
injustice, they should exhibit their petitions in the
street?
fullText: And why should we proclaim it in an hour before his entering, that if any crave redress of injustice, they should exhibit their petitions in the street?

lines: Well, I beseech you let it be proclaimed.
Betimes i' th' morn, I'll call you at your house. Give
notice to such men of sort and suit as are to meet
him.
fullText: Well, I beseech you let it be proclaimed. Betimes i' th' morn, I'll call you at your house. Give notice to such men of sort and suit as are to meet him.

lines: Good night.
This deed unshapes me quite, makes me unpregnant
And dull to all proceedings. A deflowered maid,
And by an eminent body that enforced
The law against it. But that her tender shame
Will not proclaim against her maiden loss,
How might she tongue me! Yet reason dares her no,
For my authority bears of a credent bulk
That no particular scandal once can touch
But it confounds the breather. He should have lived,
Save that his riotous youth with dangerous sense
Might in the times to come have ta'en revenge
By so receiving a dishonored life
With ransom of such shame. Would yet he had lived.
Alack, when once our grace we have forgot,
Nothing goes right. We would, and we would not.
fullText: Good night. This deed unshapes me quite, makes me unpregnant And dull to all proceedings. A deflowered maid, And by an eminent body that enforced The law against it. But that her tender shame Will not proclaim against her maiden loss, How might she tongue me! Yet reason dares her no, For my authority bears of a credent bulk That no particular scandal once can touch But it confounds the breather. He should have lived, Save that his riotous youth with dangerous sense Might in the times to come have ta'en revenge By so receiving a dishonored life With ransom of such shame. Would yet he had lived. Alack, when once our grace we have forgot, Nothing goes right. We would, and we would not.

lines: You make my bonds still greater.
fullText: You make my bonds still greater.

lines: My lord, her wits, I fear me, are not firm.
She hath been a suitor to me for her brother
Cut off by course of justice.
fullText: My lord, her wits, I fear me, are not firm. She hath been a suitor to me for her brother Cut off by course of justice.

lines: And she will speak most bitterly and strange.
fullText: And she will speak most bitterly and strange.

lines: Charges she more than me?
fullText: Charges she more than me?

lines: This is a strange abuse. Let's see thy face.
fullText: This is a strange abuse. Let's see thy face.

lines: My lord, I must confess I know this woman,
And five years since there was some speech of
marriage
Betwixt myself and her, which was broke off,
Partly for that her promised proportions
Came short of composition, but in chief
For that her reputation was disvalued
In levity. Since which time of five years
I never spake with her, saw her, nor heard from her,
Upon my faith and honor.
fullText: My lord, I must confess I know this woman, And five years since there was some speech of marriage Betwixt myself and her, which was broke off, Partly for that her promised proportions Came short of composition, but in chief For that her reputation was disvalued In levity. Since which time of five years I never spake with her, saw her, nor heard from her, Upon my faith and honor.

lines: I did but smile till now.
Now, good my lord, give me the scope of justice.
My patience here is touched. I do perceive
These poor informal women are no more
But instruments of some more mightier member
That sets them on. Let me have way, my lord,
To find this practice out.
fullText: I did but smile till now. Now, good my lord, give me the scope of justice. My patience here is touched. I do perceive These poor informal women are no more But instruments of some more mightier member That sets them on. Let me have way, my lord, To find this practice out.

lines: What can you vouch against him, Signior Lucio?
Is this the man that you did tell us of?
fullText: What can you vouch against him, Signior Lucio? Is this the man that you did tell us of?

lines: Hark how the villain would close now, after
his treasonable abuses!
fullText: Hark how the villain would close now, after his treasonable abuses!

lines: What, resists he?--Help him, Lucio.
fullText: What, resists he?--Help him, Lucio.

lines: O my dread lord,
I should be guiltier than my guiltiness
To think I can be undiscernible,
When I perceive your Grace, like power divine,
Hath looked upon my passes. Then, good prince,
No longer session hold upon my shame,
But let my trial be mine own confession.
Immediate sentence then and sequent death
Is all the grace I beg.
fullText: O my dread lord, I should be guiltier than my guiltiness To think I can be undiscernible, When I perceive your Grace, like power divine, Hath looked upon my passes. Then, good prince, No longer session hold upon my shame, But let my trial be mine own confession. Immediate sentence then and sequent death Is all the grace I beg.

lines: I was, my lord.
fullText: I was, my lord.

lines: I am sorry that such sorrow I procure;
And so deep sticks it in my penitent heart
That I crave death more willingly than mercy.
'Tis my deserving, and I do entreat it.
fullText: I am sorry that such sorrow I procure; And so deep sticks it in my penitent heart That I crave death more willingly than mercy. 'Tis my deserving, and I do entreat it.
